title: Flawed Node Depth Calculation in a Skewed Binary Tree

statement.markdown: Implement a function `solve(Node root)` that calculates the depth of each node in a binary tree. The tree can be skewed, meaning some nodes might have only one child or no children at all. The function should return a list of node depths, in the order they appear in a pre-order traversal of the tree.
statement.inputFormat: A binary tree represented as a Node class, where each node has a value, left child, and right child (all three can be `null`).
statement.outputFormat: A list of integers representing the depth of each visited node.
statement.notes: You can assume the input tree is non-empty and valid. You should not visit the same node twice.

constraints.description: Number of nodes in the binary tree

examples.explanation: Depth of each node in the given binary tree, considering pre-order traversal

hints.text: Use a pre-order traversal and a depth-first search (DFS) approach to calculate the depth of each node.

editorial.title: Optimal Solution: Pre-order Traversal with Dynamic Depth Calculation
editorial.intuition: The key insight is to calculate the depth of each node as we visit it, using a pre-order traversal. We can use a recursive DFS approach and a stack to efficiently calculate the depth of each node.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute-force approach involves calculating the height of each node separately, which would result in O(n^2) time complexity.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use a recursive DFS approach to calculate the depth of each node, along with a stack to keep track of nodes to visit.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a pre-order traversal with dynamic depth calculation, resulting in O(n) time complexity.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: We can prove the correctness of the solution by considering the properties of a binary tree and the pre-order traversal strategy.
editorial.dryRun: A step-by-step example run can be provided to illustrate the solution process.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly calculating the depth of each node
Failing to handle skewed binary trees

solutions.python:
from collections import deque
class Solution:
    def solve(self, root):
        maxDepth = 0
        result = []
        if not root:
            return result
        q = collections.deque([root])
        q.append(None)
        while q:
            node = q.popleft()
            if not node:
                maxDepth += 1
                q.append(None)
            else:
                result.append(maxDepth)
                q.append(node.left)
                q.append(node.right)
        return result
print(Solution().solve(Node(1, Node(2), Node(3))))  # [1]
print(Solution().solve(Node(1, Node(2, Node(4), Node(5)), Node(3, Node(6))))))  # [1, 2, 1, 2, 1]
